title: Chapter 38: The Reckoning
content: Six months is long enough to watch an empire rot from the inside.

The Palais de Justice sat on the Île de la Cité like a stone gargoyle with its mouth open, swallowing people into limestone corridors that smelled of floor wax and old paper and the faintest ghost of cigarettes smoked by clerks who should have known better. Paris Criminal Court. Room 7B. The ceiling was high and fussy, all gilded moulding and republican pretension, and the light that filtered through the tall windows had the flat, impartial quality of judgment itself.
Edward Rochester sat in the defendant's chair, and Jane did not recognize him.

Not at first. Not the way you recognize someone you once loved — the way your body knows before your mind catches up. He had lost weight. The jaw that had seemed carved from Carrara marble in every Thornfield campaign was now almost gaunt, cheekbones pressing against skin that had lost its Mediterranean gilt. His hair was shorter, neater, the silver at the temples no longer styled into rakish distinction but simply allowed to exist, as if vanity itself had been entered into evidence and struck from the record.

He wore a navy suit. Not Tom Ford. Not even Brioni. Something modest and off-the-rack, the kind of concession a defense attorney would coach a client into — *Look humble, Edward. Look like a man who has already suffered.* His hands rested on the table before him, still. No signet ring. No watch that cost more than a car. His fingernails were cropped short.
But his eyes.
His eyes were the same.

They moved through the courtroom like a cashier counting a till — plaintiff's counsel, judge, jury box, gallery, press section — cataloguing, assessing, calculating odds. Jane had watched those eyes undress a room a hundred times in a hundred boardrooms and ballrooms and bedrooms across Europe, and the performance of humility did not reach them. Everything else was costume. The navy suit, the shorter hair, the still hands. Costume. His eyes were Edward's eyes: cold, fast, and always, always working the angle.
The prosecution rose.

Maître Valérie Duval was a compact woman in her fifties with close-cropped grey hair and the sort of voice that filled a room without rising — the voice of someone who had spent thirty years making juries feel that justice was not abstract but physical, something you could reach out and touch. She did not raise her chin or square her shoulders for emphasis. She simply spoke, and the room bent toward her.

"The People present four articles of charge against Edward Fairfax Rochester." She held up four fingers. Not theatrical. Clinical. "One: financial fraud, including securities manipulation, embezzlement of corporate funds, and systematic tax evasion across six jurisdictions, totaling no less than three hundred and forty million euros over a period of eight years." Her first finger folded down. "Two: false imprisonment — the unlawful confinement of Bertha Antoinetta Mason within the grounds of Thornfield SA properties under falsified medical authorization." A second finger. "Three: medical abuse — the coercion, procurement, and administration of psychiatric medication for the purpose of silencing a legal spouse and concealing the existence of a marriage from business partners and the public." A third. "And four: aggravated trafficking in persons — the systematic exploitation of supply chain labor across Thornfield's South Asian and North African manufacturing operations, including the withholding of wages, confiscation of travel documents, and confinement of workers in conditions constituting forced labor under Article 225-4-1 of the French Penal Code."

The courtroom was so quiet Jane could hear the tick of the clock above the judge's bench, could hear the scratch of the court reporter's keys, could hear the man two rows behind her shift his weight and the leather seat creak like a small confession.

Duval's evidence was methodical and it was devastating. Financial records subpoenaed from seven banks across three continents. Internal Thornfield communications — the kind that happened on encrypted servers and satellite phones, the kind Edward had believed untraceable — decrypted and laid bare by the joint Franco-British investigative team. Medical files from three facilities, the forged consent forms, Dr. Fournier's own handwritten notes — *Patient displays resistance to protocol. Recommend increased dosage and restricted visitation.* — entered into evidence with the dry presentation of a coroner's report.

And the supply chain footage. God, the footage. Shot by an undercover journalist in a Dhaka finishing house, it showed workers sleeping under their sewing machines, a locked dormitory with bars on the windows, a foreman collecting passports in a desk drawer. The label on the garments was visible in every frame: THORNFIELD.

Jane's stomach turned. She had worn those garments. She had designed complementary pieces for those garments. She had believed — because Edward had told her, because the brand playbook said so, because it was easier to believe — that Thornfield's supply chain was spotless. Audited. Certified. Ethical.
The word *ethical* tasted like ash in her mouth.

Edward sat through all of it without moving. Without speaking. Without — and this was the detail that made Jane's skin crawl — flinching. He watched the supply chain footage with the same expression he'd worn watching a runway show: assessing cut, construction, effect. Not horror. Not shame. Assessment.
And then it was Bertha's turn.

She entered through the witness door, not the gallery, and the room shifted. The press section straightened. The jury consciously uncrossed and recrossed their arms. Even the judge, who had presided over this case for three weeks with the imperturbable calm of a woman who had seen everything, looked up from her notes.

Bertha Antoinetta Mason wore a simple navy dress. Knee-length. Three-quarter sleeves. No jewelry except small gold studs in her ears. Her hair was pulled back in a low twist. She looked like what she was: a woman who had survived something that should have killed her, and who had decided, at some point between the locked rooms and the forced medication and the long dark nights when she wasn't sure if she was losing her mind or if her mind was being stolen from her, that she would not be erased.

The punk tuxedo — that magnificent, sequined, two-fingered salute to the industry that had tried to bury her — that was for the world stage. The Met. The Cairo Fashion Summit. The cover of *Vogue*. That was armor for war. This was something different. This was testimony. This was the record. She had dressed for the permanence of the transcript.
Maître Duval approached the witness box with a nod. "Madame Mason, can you state for the record your medical history as it pertains to this case?"

Bertha's voice was steady. Low. Caribbean vowels still present, still hers, softened but never erased by the years. "I was diagnosed with bipolar II disorder in 2014. I have managed it for twelve years with a combination of mood stabilizers, therapy, and lifestyle protocols. I have never been hospitalized involuntarily. I have never been a danger to myself or others. My condition is real." A pause. She looked at the jury — not for sympathy, but for clarity. "His weaponization of it was the crime."
The courtroom exhaled.

Duval walked her through the timeline. The marriage in 2018 — a secret ceremony in Switzerland, kept from the board, kept from the public, kept from the financial disclosures that required spousal assets to be declared. The first episode of confinement in 2020 at a private clinic outside Lyon, where Bertha had gone voluntarily for a medication adjustment and been held for eleven weeks under Dr. Fournier's authorization — authorization Edward had procured by presenting himself as her legal guardian rather than her spouse. The second confinement in 2022. The third.
"And during these confinements," Duval said, "were you administered medication?"
"Yes."
"Were you consulted about the medication?"
"No. I was told it was standard protocol."
"Was it standard protocol for your diagnosis?"

"No. The dosages were — " Bertha's composure flickered, just barely, a micro-tremor in her jaw that she controlled with visible effort. "The dosages were consistent with sedation, not treatment. I spent most of each day unconscious. I lost eleven weeks of memory from the second confinement. Eleven weeks. That's not treatment. That's erasure."

The defense cross-examined. Maître Renard was smooth, well-prepared, and careful — he knew the jury was watching, and bullying a composed Black woman in a navy dress would play exactly as badly as it sounded. He tried to suggest alternative interpretations of the medical records, to introduce reasonable doubt about the voluntariness of Fournier's compliance, to construct a scaffolding of ambiguity around the word *protocol*.
Bertha dismantled him with patience. Not anger. Not tears. Patience — the kind that comes from having nothing left to prove and everything left to say.
And then came the mirror.

Helen Burns had been working in the shadows for months — Jane had known this, had seen the late-night encrypted messages, the files that multiplied on Helen's laptop like cells dividing, the way Helen's eyes went distant and focused when she was following a thread Jane couldn't yet see. What Jane hadn't known, what nobody had known until Duval presented it on the courtroom's screen, was how deep the thread went.
"Céline Laurent," Duval said, and clicked to a photograph.

The woman on the screen was beautiful in the way fashion people meant when they said *interesting* — sharp features, dark circles under her eyes that read as aesthetic rather than exhaustion, a gaze that looked past the camera at something it couldn't quite reach. A French-Algerian designer who had shown two collections at Paris Fashion Week before dying by suicide in 2019 at the age of thirty-one.

"Céline Laurent was a designer under contract with Thornfield's now-defunct subsidiary, Maison Lumière," Duval continued. "In 2018, she was referred to Dr. Pierre Fournier by Mr. Rochester's personal assistant. She was prescribed the same medication protocol as Madame Mason. She was subject to the same legal framework — a confinement authorization signed by Dr. Fournier and countersigned by Mr. Rochester, designating him as her medical proxy. She was confined to the same clinic outside Lyon for six weeks in the spring of 2019. Four months after her release, she was found dead in her apartment. The original ruling was suicide."
Duval paused. The courtroom air was thick, almost viscous, the way air gets when two hundred people are breathing the same shock.

"The People submit that Céline Laurent was not a tragic outlier. She was a test case. Dr. Fournier's experimental protocol — the sedation-heavy regimen, the confinement under fabricated authority, the isolation from family and counsel — was first deployed on Mademoiselle Laurent. When it produced the desired compliance, it was scaled. Refined. Applied to Madame Mason." Duval turned to face the jury. "The architecture of Bertha Mason's imprisonment was built on Céline Laurent's grave."

The photograph of Céline Laurent remained on the screen for five full seconds. The woman looking past the camera. The woman who had been the blueprint for a crime so systematic it could have been a business plan — because for Edward Rochester, it *was* a business plan. A legal architecture. A construction of paperwork and pharmaceuticals and coerced signatures, as designed and as deliberate as any garment that came out of the Thornfield atelier.

Jane felt something crack inside her — not break, crack, the way ice cracks in spring, a sound that is also a promise — and she understood that she had been wearing Céline Laurent's death without knowing it. Every day she had worked at Thornfield, every day she had believed in its beauty and its craft, she had been walking on ground that was also a grave.
The jury deliberated for four hours.

Four hours for a trial that had lasted three weeks and produced forty-seven volumes of evidence. Four hours for financial fraud, false imprisonment, medical abuse, and aggravated trafficking. Four hours for the full weight of a system designed by one man to consume people and convert them into profit and silence.
It felt like both an eternity and an instant.
The foreman stood. A retired schoolteacher from the Fourth Arrondissement with reading glasses on a chain around her neck and the steady voice of someone who had spent decades announcing grades.
"Guilty."
"Guilty."
"Guilty."
"Guilty."
On all counts.

The presiding judge — Madame La Présidente Legrand, who had not once raised her voice in three weeks — delivered the sentence with the same measured cadence. Eighteen years. Two hundred and forty million euros in fines and restitution, to be distributed among defrauded investors, supply chain workers through a court-supervised reparations fund, and the estate of Céline Laurent. Thornfield SA into immediate receivership under court-appointed oversight, all assets frozen pending liquidation or restructuring.

Edward Rochester stood as the gendarmes approached. He did not resist. He did not shout. He did not weep. He held out his wrists for the handcuffs with a kind of mechanical compliance, as if his body had simply accepted what his mind was already working past.
And then he looked at Jane.

She was seated in the third row of the gallery, near the aisle, close enough to see every muscle in his face. She expected rage — the Edward she knew would have raged, would have burned, would have made the courtroom itself feel the heat of his fury. She expected remorse — some part of her, the part that had loved him, still wanted to believe that some part of him was capable of it.
She got neither.

His eyes met hers, and what she saw in them was — calculation. Measurement. The rapid, reflexive appraisal of a man considering a chessboard after a lost game. Not *how do I feel about this* but *what is the move from here*. He was already thinking about appeals, about sentence reductions, about jurisdictional technicalities, about the friend he had in the Ministry, about the account in Singapore that no one had found yet. She could see it. The gears were turning even as the handcuffs clicked shut, even as the gendarmes' hands closed on his arms, even as he was led toward the door at the side of the courtroom that would take him down a corridor and into a van and away.
Even in handcuffs, Edward Rochester was looking for the angle.

The door closed behind him. The courtroom erupted — press surging for the exit, spectators murmuring, counsel gathering papers. Jane sat still. She could hear it all — the scrape of chairs, the rustle of coats, the frantic tap-tap-tap of reporters filing their leads — and she could hear, underneath it, her own breathing. Steady. Present. Alive.

She stood. She walked through the limestone corridors with their floor wax and old paper and ghost-cigarettes, past the clusters of journalists, past the cameras that swung toward her and then away when they realized she wasn't going to stop, through the heavy wooden doors and out into the Paris afternoon.

The light hit her face. Sun through high clouds, that particular Parisian light that makes every Haussmann façade glow like it's been polished, that makes the Seine look like hammered silver. The air smelled of river water and diesel and roasted chestnuts from a cart on the quai, and somewhere a bicycle bell rang twice, and a child laughed, and a woman argued with her phone in rapid Arabic, and the world — the ordinary, daily, indifferent world — kept turning.
Jane Eyre walked down the steps of the Palais de Justice, and she was wearing her own clothes.

Not Thornfield. Not anyone's label. Vintage Levi's that she'd bought at a market in Berlin three years ago, soft and faded to the exact shade of a summer sky. A Yohji Yamamoto jacket — oversized, black, architectural — she'd found at a consignment shop in Marais, because the shape pleased her and the fabric moved like water and it didn't have a single person's name stamped on it claiming ownership of her silhouette. Boots from a flea market in Kreuzberg, scuffed and perfect. No label. No brand. No signature but her own.

The crimson dress — *that* dress, the one he had given her, the one she had worn like a dare and a trap and a longing she couldn't name — was hanging in her London closet. Not discarded. Not burned. Kept. Because she had earned the right to remember what it had cost her to wear it, and the right to decide, every single morning, not to put it on again. The dress was a reminder. Not of him. Of who she had been when she believed that being chosen by someone powerful was the same thing as being free.

She reached the quai and stopped at the railing. The river moved below her — grey-green and unhurried, carrying a barge and a pair of swans and the reflected clouds toward the sea. The chestnut vendor's fire crackled. The child was still laughing, somewhere behind her, inside the parenthesis of an ordinary afternoon.
Quiet luxury? No, baby. It's just noisy hypocrisy dressed in cashmere.

Jane closed her eyes. The sun was warm on her face. The air smelled of chestnuts and river and the faint green scent of the horse chestnut trees along the embankment. She could hear the city — all of it, the beauty and the noise and the thousand small violences of living — and she could hear herself inside it, and the two sounds no longer contradicted each other.
She opened her eyes.
Free.
Truly, finally, herself.
